--- SOURCE 1: Will Rising Costs and Labor Shortages Push 3D Printing ... ---
URL: https://propmodo.com/will-rising-costs-and-labor-shortages-push-3d-printing-into-the-mainstream/

SUMMARY:
Rising costs, labor shortages, and housing demand are pushing the construction industry toward faster, cheaper methods—like 3D printing.

FULL CONTENT:

![Propmodo](https://propmodo.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/propmodo-logo-blue-decoding.svg)

![](https://propmodo.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/3d-printing-building.jpg)

# Will Rising Costs and Labor Shortages Push 3D Printing Into the Mainstream?

The construction industry is under a lot of pressure right now. On one hand is the pressure to build. The U.S. is experiencing a growing housing shortage that has created an opportunity for builders who can produce the much needed homes. On the other hand is the economic and political situation. High interest rates have made construction loans prohibitively expensive. Tariffs have already made important construction materials like steel more expensive and threaten to raise the prices of many other necessary building components as well. A crackdown on undocumented workers also looms. By [some estimates](https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2024/10/21/most-us-voters-say-immigrants-no-matter-their-legal-status-mostly-take-jobs-citizens-dont-want/), around 13 percent of all construction workers in the U.S. are likely undocumented. Losing a part of that workforce combined with the large number of older workers who are set to retire in the next few years will create a shortage that will make hiring trade workers even more expensive.

All of these forces are pushing the construction industry to find a way to build faster, cheaper, and with less labor. The pressure might eventually produce a diamond in the form of 3D printed buildings. 3D print construction, also called additive construction, has been around for a while. There have been many pilot projects that have printed houses, [small offices](https://newatlas.com/worlds-first-3d-printed-office-dubai/38253/), and even a [drive-through Starbucks](https://apnews.com/article/starbucks-first-3d-printed-store-texas-552972e9df63796e42ede152747b9069). But despite the obvious advantages of 3D printing, most of the 3D printed construction was limited to small, one off projects. Now 3D printing technology has improved making it a viable option for the construction industry.

One of the deterrents to 3D printed construction are earthquakes. Buildings in seismically active areas need to adhere to certain construction standards. Designs for buildings need to be tested to see how they would stand up to earthquakes of different types and sizes. 3D printed buildings often rely on the poured material for their structure, many don’t have steel or engineered wood frames to help support their weight. That creates a problem since there isn’t much uniformity yet in the type of materials used for 3D printing. Each different manufacturer needs to test out their materials under different conditions.

To help clear 3D printed homes for earthquake prone areas, [researchers are starting to test 3D printed structures](https://www.imeche.org/news/news-article/simulated-earthquake-puts-3d-printed-concrete-'house'-to-the-test) on sophisticated “shake tables.” The tables can test how different methods and geometries would perform in earthquakes of increasing magnitude. Sensors on the shake table can identify where problems occur in a way that would allow for design changes to address any weaknesses. One of the advantages of 3D printed construction is that designs can be changed as easily as a CAD file since the robot printer will diligently reproduce any digital plans provided to it.

“This experiment aims to fill the knowledge gap surrounding the dynamic response of 3D-printed units, particularly how they perform under recorded and simulated seismic events,” said Dr. Raffaele De Risi, the head of the shake table project. “By doing so, the team aims to identify strengths, weaknesses and failure mechanisms specific to this construction method.”

There have already been some innovative new techniques that have helped 3D printed buildings reach new heights. Recently the world’s largest 3D printed tower was built in a small town in Switzerland. The structure, called Tor Alva (White Tower in English) is 30 meters high and uses ornate, spiraling columns to support its own weight. The researchers behind the team were able to achieve this kind of height by using a second robot that would place round metal wires between some of the layers of concrete that help hold it together. Each story was then held together using wires that were passed through designated channels built into the design. The tower is meant to not only showcase what 3D printing can achieve, height wise, it also highlights another aspect of 3D printed structures: modularity. Tor Alva was built in a town with just 11 inhabitants and is designed to be taken apart and moved to other small towns in the area to help build cultural identity and boost tourism.

For 3D printing to really catch on there needs to be proof that 3D printed buildings are both economic and desirable. There are a few large developments that are doing exactly that. In Texas one of the largest 3D printed communities has been completed. Wolf Ranch is a collaboration between Lennar, BIG and the construction technology company ICON. It is comprised of 100 homes that come with not only 3D printed walls but lots of other smart home features. Over a quarter of the homes have already been sold between $450,000 and $600,000 (the median home price in the area is $450,00), proving that there is a market for these types of homes.

Another large 3D printed development is being constructed in B.C., Canada. The homes are more affordable than others built in the traditional way and can be erected in merely a few days. As more areas battle with housing affordability and more government incentives are designated to help spur more affordable development, there will be more focus on using 3D printing as a cheaper, faster way to build homes.

One of the biggest barriers to 3D printing isn’t technology, it is industry pushback. Contractors have used the same construction techniques for decades and are not willing to change to a completely new, unknown system. Many construction workers don’t even have the necessary skills to be able to use 3D printers. [Recent research](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44290-024-00132-5) shows that digital tools are not being adopted by the construction industry because of insufficient training and perceived technological complexity. As much as 3D printing advocates hope that this new method will be adopted for its obvious benefits, it is more likely that 3D printing will have to fit into our current building methods for it to catch on. The more pressure that is put on the construction industry the more it will be forced to change. That need for change could be the thing that finally helps push 3D printing out of the fringe of building and into the mainstream.

--- SOURCE 3: How Is 3D Printing Affecting The Construction Industry? ---

SUMMARY:
For starters, it can reduce costs for labor and materials. Printing building components on-site mitigates issues such as rising costs and supply

FULL CONTENT:
![Mauldin & Jenkins](https://www.mjcpa.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/cropped-cropped-Mauldin_Jenkins_stacked_color-resized.png)
![You are currently viewing How is 3D printing affecting the construction industry?](https://www.mjcpa.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/04/04_01_25_2235617135_CB_560x292.jpg "How is 3D printing affecting the construction industry? Mauldin & Jenkins")

# How is 3D printing affecting the construction industry?

If you’re even remotely interested in technology, you’ve likely encountered the term “3D printing.” Sometimes also referred to as “additive manufacturing,” it’s the process of creating three-dimensional objects from digital designs by thinly layering materials over time with a special printer.

3D printing has been gradually gaining traction in the construction industry. Does that mean you should run out and buy one of those special printers today? Probably not. However, it’s essential to be aware of the benefits and challenges of this exciting new technology.

Benefits for building

On the plus side, 3D printing offers intriguing benefits for construction companies. For starters, it can reduce costs for labor and materials. Printing building components on-site mitigates issues such as rising costs and supply chain slowdowns that have plagued the construction industry in recent years. And it helps eliminate wasted materials because everything is printed to spec.

In fact, the design options are nearly limitless, allowing for innovative and often more energy-efficient structures. Much of the process is automated, too, which should save considerable time.

Another potential advantage is that fewer workers are typically needed to assemble 3D-printed components. Thus, the technology can help ease the ongoing skilled labor shortage. Also, because of the highly automated nature of 3D printing, fewer jobsite injuries are likely to occur.

Challenges to overcome

On the other hand, 3D printing faces various challenges. First, there’s a lack of regulation. Traditional building codes generally aren’t designed to address 3D-printed structures. Until the regulatory environment catches up with the tech, a great deal of uncertainty over compliance, liability and other legal ramifications will likely hamper its use.

Second, as of this writing, limited compatible building materials exist. Most 3D printers work with some forms of concrete, plastics, metals and composites. However, they’re not much use for jobs that call for other materials, such as most types of wood, traditional reinforced concrete with rebar, conventional brick and mortar, and glass.

Third, 3D printers with the necessary functionality are expensive, as are the software programs that run them. And using a 3D printer on a jobsite involves substantial transportation, setup and dismantling costs. This isn’t like a drone, where you can buy a reasonably affordable model, undergo some training and deploy the asset. A 3D printer, along with its associated software and training, represents a substantial capital expense for most small to midsize construction businesses.

Real-world applications

If you’re curious about how 3D printing is being used on real-world projects in the United States today, there are various examples. In January of this year, construction technologies company ICON completed three 3D-printed barracks for the U.S. Army at Fort Bliss, Texas. This publicly funded project was notable because the design complied with the U.S. Department of Defense’s military-grade Unified Facilities Criteria.

On the commercial side, in 2024, retail giant Walmart partnered with construction tech business Alquist 3D to build an 8,000-square-foot store expansion in Athens, Georgia. Although the project was completed, it ultimately highlighted the challenges of 3D printing in construction, as the job brought struggles with materials issues and environmental conditions that drove up costs.

Something to keep an eye on

Whether and how 3D printing will affect your construction company depends on factors such as your specialty and market. As mentioned, you probably don’t need to invest in a printer hastily. However, it’s something to keep an eye on. We can help you assess the total costs and return on investment of any technology you’re considering.

# How 3D Printing Is Redefining the Construction Industry

3D printing, also known as additive manufacturing, is no longer a futuristic concept; it’s an active force reshaping how we design, build, and think about the built environment—one layer at a time. With the global push toward automation, sustainability, and cost-efficiency, 3D printing in construction is rapidly moving from novelty to necessity.

Using traditional methods, building a home or office complex can take months—sometimes years. But with large-scale 3D printers, entire structures can be printed in days using specially formulated concrete or composite materials. These printers operate by depositing material layer-by-layer, following a digital blueprint with extreme precision.

### Why 3D Printing Matters for the Future of Construction

The impact of 3D printing technology in construction is multifaceted. Here’s why the industry is taking notice:

**Speed and Efficiency**  
With 3D printing, structures can be built significantly faster than with conventional methods. Reduced manual labor, fewer supply chain dependencies, and 24/7 operability make it a time-saver—especially critical in post-disaster or remote-area housing.

**Cost Reduction**  
By minimizing material waste and reducing labor costs, 3D printed construction can lower the overall expense of building projects. For affordable housing initiatives, this could be a game-changer.

**Design Freedom**  
Architects and engineers are no longer confined by the limitations of traditional building materials. 3D printing allows for more complex geometries, enabling the creation of previously unbuildable designs.

**Sustainability**  
A lot of 3D printing technologies utilize locally sourced or recycled materials. Reduced transportation emissions, minimal waste, and the potential integration of smart building features make this a more eco-conscious option.

**Labor and Safety Benefits**  
With ongoing labor shortages in construction, automation provides a practical solution. At the same time, automated building processes reduce worker exposure to hazardous conditions on-site.

### What’s Next?

As construction innovation accelerates, we can expect an increase in hybrid models—where 3D printing complements traditional building methods. Integration with robotics, AI, and BIM (Building Information Modeling) will create a more connected, data-driven construction ecosystem.

As the industry evolves, embracing construction technology like 3D printing isn’t just about staying ahead—it’s about building smarter, faster, and more sustainably. For stakeholders across the value chain—from architects and engineers to developers and city planners—now is the time to explore how additive manufacturing can unlock new value for your business.

IMAGE 3:

DESCRIPTION: A construction worker in a safety vest and helmet observes a 3D-printed concrete wall as a robotic arm deposits material onto a cylindrical column, illustrating the use of 3D printing technology to address labor shortages in the construction industry.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://pepuphome.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/02/3D-Printing-in-Construction-Industry-Benefits-Challenges-3.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A large 3D printer is actively constructing a building framework with concrete components on a construction site, surrounded by safety barriers and multiple workers preparing various tasks.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://interestingengineering.com/_next/image?url=https://images.interestingengineering.com/2023/06/11/image/jpeg/I5wcv9ezq0BSq9w4MgwlM9oWtSmql1Vdn2aTrMj6.jpg&w=1200&q=75
DESCRIPTION: A humanoid robot wearing a yellow construction helmet holds a yellow power drill in front of a concrete wall, suggesting automation in construction.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: The construction sector faces significant labor shortages due to retiring workers and immigration crackdowns, prompting a shift towards automated solutions like 3D printing.
3D printing can utilize recycled materials and reduce waste, but it still faces scrutiny over energy consumption and the environmental impact of specific printing materials.
Existing building codes often do not address 3D-printed structures, creating uncertainty regarding compliance and liability, which hinders widespread adoption in the construction industry.
